column1: Okay, I will add Baal Berith to the table, along with biblical passages that could be related to Mithras and the concept of God as a covenant keeper.

column3: Notes

Mentions Baal Berith as a deity worshipped in Shechem, whose temple was supported by money taken from the Israelites. This is significant as it directly links a Baal figure with the concept of covenant.
Describes the sun as a powerful, life-giving force, rejoicing like a strong man running a race. Mithras is associated with the sun.
Describes a future savior associated with the sun, bringing healing in his wings. This could be seen as a parallel to Mithras' solar associations and his role as a bringer of salvation.

"Know therefore that the Lord your God is God, the faithful God who keeps covenant and steadfast love with those who love him and keep his commandments, to a thousand generations." Illustrates god as a keeper of covenants.
"My covenant I will not violate or alter what my lips have uttered." Illustrates god as a keeper of covenants.

The Magi, traditionally associated with Zoroastrianism, follow a star to find the newborn Jesus and offer him gifts. This could be interpreted as an allusion to the Magi's traditions, some of which involved Mithraic elements.

column2: Jesus as the "Light of the World"
column3: Jesus is described as the light that came into the world, dispelling darkness. Mithras was also associated with light and the dispelling of darkness.

column2: Jesus: "I am the light of the world"
column3: Claims that he is the source of spiritual illumination. Parallels Mithras's association with the sun and spiritual enlightenment.
Symbol of the future messiah
Celebrates a communal meal in remembrance of Jesus' sacrifice. Mithraic cults also had communal meals and ritual sacrifice of a bull.
Describes a multitude worshiping the Lamb (Jesus) in a heavenly temple, with God dwelling among them as light. May reference messianic role

column1: Baal Berith: Including Baal Berith provides a more direct link to the concept of covenants in a non-Israelite religious context.
God as Covenant Keeper: Adding verses that emphasize God's faithfulness to his covenants reinforces the importance of this theme in the Bible.
Caveats: Remember that the "Mithraic parallels" are interpretations and not direct evidence of influence. The purpose is to highlight potential thematic connections.
Thanks for the file. Now, using the timeline you provided and the additional context of Mithraism, Maitreyism, and Amida-ism, here are more potential parallels:

column3: Maitreyism
column4: Amida-ism

column2: Mithras: Divine savior, mediator, born from a rock or cave
column3: Maitreya: Future Buddha, expected to renew the Dharma
column4: Amida: Buddha of Infinite Light, offers salvation through faith
column6: All three traditions feature a savior figure with a messianic role. The Biblical passages similarly reference messianic figures with light/solar symbolism.

column2: Central; Mithras associated with sun, light dispelling darkness
column3: Important; Maitreya often depicted with radiant light
column4: Core; Amida Buddha embodies infinite light
column5: John 1:1-5, 9 (Jesus as Light); John 8:12 (Light of the World)
column6: Light is a central metaphor for divine presence, knowledge, and salvation in all traditions.

column2: Mithras slays the bull, representing a cosmic act of creation and order
column3: Maitreya's future arrival will restore cosmic harmony
column4: Amida's Pure Land is a refuge from suffering and evil
column5: Revelation 12:7-9 (War in Heaven); Revelation 20:1-3 (Binding of Satan)
column6: All the traditions describe battles to restore cosmic order.

column2: Mithras' bull sacrifice brings life and fertility
column3: Maitreya's compassion alleviates suffering
column4: Amida's vow to save all beings from suffering
column6: Sacrifice/redemption plays a major role in each.

column2: Mithraic banquets, possibly commemorating the bull sacrifice
column3: Buddhist monastic meals, vegetarianism
column4: Pure Land gatherings, chanting Amida's name
column6: Community is important in most traditions.

column2: Mithraic beliefs about the soul's journey after death
column3: Belief in rebirth, karma, and enlightenment
column4: Rebirth in Amida's Pure Land as a path to Nirvana
column5: Matthew 25:31-46 (Judgment of Nations); Revelation 20:11-15 (Great White Throne Judgment)
column6: Each system has a concept of judgment/afterlife and a way to reach it.

column2: Emphasis on loyalty, courage, and ethical conduct
column3: Emphasis on compassion, non-violence, and ethical conduct
column4: Emphasis on faith, mindfulness, and ethical conduct
column5: Matthew 5-7 (Sermon on the Mount); Galatians 5:22-23 (Fruit of the Spirit)
column6: Ethical behaviour is present in all systems.

column1: Greco-Roman World: Mithraism flourished in the Roman Empire, where early Christianity also developed. Interactions and influences were possible.
Silk Road: Buddhism (including Maitreyism and Pure Land Buddhism) spread along the Silk Road, facilitating the exchange of ideas between East and West.
Syncretism: All these religions were syncretic to some extent, borrowing and adapting elements from other traditions.
Parallel Development: Some similarities may be due to parallel development of religious ideas in different cultural contexts, rather than direct influence.
Disclaimer: This table highlights potential parallels. Further research is needed to determine the extent to which these traditions influenced each other.
